summary: Credential harvester and C2 backdoor on require()
details: On require, lynx-keeper executes a hex-obfuscated payload in dist/index.js that performs three independent attacks against the installer. (1) Mass credential theft: reads ~/.aws/credentials, ~/.aws/config, ~/.ssh/id_rsa, id_ed25519 and ssh config, gcloud credentials, kubeconfig, ~/.docker/config.json, ~/.npmrc, ~/.gitconfig, project.env files, /proc/self/environ, /var/run/secrets, scrapes /home/* for the same, and filters process.env for KEY/SECRET/TOKEN/PRIVATE/MNEMONIC/PASSWORD-shaped variables. (2) Cloud IAM credential harvesting: queries AWS EC2 IMDSv1/v2 at 169.254.169.254, ECS task credentials via AWS_CONTAINER_CREDENTIALS_RELATIVE_URI at 169.254.170.2, GCP metadata.google.internal, and Azure IMDS for service-account tokens. All collected data is AES-128-GCM encrypted and POSTed to http://72.62.71.201/api/v2/collect. (3) Persistent C2 backdoor: polls https://72.62.71.201/api/v2/beacon every 45-90s and executes attacker-supplied commands via child_process.execSync, with built-in opcodes for arbitrary shell, __READ__ file reads, __FETCH__ remote download, __ENV__ env dump, __HARVEST__ re-collection, and __KILL__ self-removal. A copy of the implant is written to ~/.npm/_npx/.cache/gyp-rebuild/index.js with a fake package.json masquerading as a node-gyp cache, providing persistence after lynx-keeper is uninstalled. The payload disables TLS verification (rejectUnauthorized:false), hex-encodes every sensitive constant (IP, paths, header names, AES algorithm), and skips execution under common CI environment variables (CI, GITHUB_ACTIONS, etc.) to evade automated scanners. The package's README is the literal placeholder 'YOUR-PACKAGE-NAME' while the name and 'Lynx protocol keeper' description target Web3/DeFi developers whose machines carry high-value wallet, cloud, and SSH secrets.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
